card.intuition: Place one pointer at each end of a sorted array and move them toward each other to avoid the O(n²) brute-force double loop.
card.analogy: Two people walking toward each other from opposite ends of a hallway — they meet in the middle far faster than one person searching every spot.
when_to_use: Array is sorted (or can be sorted) and you need pairs/triplets summing to a target
Checking if a string or array is a palindrome
Merging two sorted arrays or removing duplicates in-place
Container-with-most-water style problems (maximize area between two indices)
gotchas: Two pointers requires the array to be sorted — if it isn't, sort it first (O(n log n)) or use a hash set instead
Be careful about when to move lo vs hi: move lo when the current sum is too small, hi when too large
For triplet problems, fix one element and run two-pointer on the remainder — remember to skip duplicates

exercise.prompt: Complete two-pointer two-sum on a sorted array. Return the pair of indices (lo, hi) that sum to target, or None.
hints: Sum too small → the left pointer must move right to increase the smaller value
Sum too large → the right pointer must move left to decrease the larger value
exercise.test_code:
assert two_sum_sorted([1,2,3,4,6], 6)  == (1,3), 'Expected (1,3) for 2+4=6'
assert two_sum_sorted([2,7,11,15], 9) == (0,1), 'Expected (0,1) for 2+7=9'
assert two_sum_sorted([1,3,5,7,9], 12) == (1,4), 'Expected (1,4) for 3+9=12'
assert two_sum_sorted([1,2,3], 10)    is None,  'Expected None — no pair sums to 10'
assert two_sum_sorted([5,5], 10)       == (0,1), 'Duplicates: 5+5=10'
print('PASS')
